title: Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis
description:
A risk-assessment framework to identify sustainable management actions for the jumbo flying squid (Dosidicus gigas) in the South-Eastern Pacific Ocean. It applies reference-point-based management quantities inside a probabilistic setup that accommodates multiple, competing hypotheses drawn from the stock-assessment models developed under the South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation (SPRFMO).

Three SPRFMO parties presented stock-assessment methodologies for jumbo flying squid to the 2025 Scientific Committee: the CALAMASUR approach (a monthly, fleet-specific depletion model scaled up to a production model), the Chilean SPiCT model (annual CPUE indices, frequentist maximum likelihood), and the Chinese model (fully Bayesian, GAM-standardised CPUE in a state-space model). These assessments reach markedly different conclusions despite overlapping data from the same fishery.

Rather than selecting a single "best" model, the framework carries the competing models as weighted hypotheses: identifying alternative hypotheses about population dynamics, weighting them by model reliability and expert judgement, and combining the resulting distributions into probability statements that can directly inform harvest control rules.

This record archives the project website, which includes the working paper, per-model summaries, and the presentation given to the SPRFMO Third Squid Workshop (SCW18).
